details.text:
لَا أُقْسِمُ بِهَٰذَا الْبَلَدِ

- “No! I swear by this city” – Concerning the use of the oath here then it can be seen as a way of honouring or raising the standing of the matter the oath is being sworn by i.e., ٱلْبَلَدِ (The city i.e., Makkah). It is also a way in the Arabic language to grab the attention of those being addressed for what is about to be said. In addition, that which is about to be stated has a connection to that which you have sworn by.

- لَا أُقْسِمُ is one of the ways that Allah swears an oath in the Qur’an. The oath of أُقْسِمُ "I swear" is always preceded by لاَ “No!” when used in the Qur’an when the One swearing the oath is Allah. The لا is not stated to negate the verb of أُقْسِمُ (“I swear”) but is rather in response to a prior or existing counter argument or belief to negate that. Before making the oath, Allah is saying: “No” to negate all other ideas.

Derivative: النَّجْدَيْنِ – نَجْد refers to a wide path on highland or elevated land. Here, النَّجْدَيْنِ the two paths refer to the paths of good and evil, truth and falsehood, or guidance and misguidance.

وَهَدَيْنَاهُ النَّجْدَيْنِ

“And we guided him (وَهَدَيْنَاهُ) the two paths (النَّجْدَيْنِ)” means that We showed and made clear to the human being the two paths i.e., of good and evil.
Derivative: الْعَقَبَةَ refers to a rugged mountain path. Here, this path is being described as the path to salvation.

Derivative: اقْتَحَمَ – اِقْتِحام means to jump, rush or plunge into something without considerations. It can also mean to enter something with force and strength without considerations. The meaning here is to struggle

فَلَا اقْتَحَمَ الْعَقَبَةَ

- The use of لَا with the past tense of اقْتَحَمَ instead of the usual مَا can indicate to the fact that the matter being negated has a number of elements connected to it as لَا can be used instead of مَا to indicate to the presence of multiple elements. It would indicate here that الْعَقَبَةَ (the difficult and rugged mountain pass or path) has numerous elements to it. لَا has also been said to imply a question here where the meaning would be: “Why has he not plunged in and engaged in the struggle to climb the rugged mountain path” implying that this is what he should have done.

- “What will make you know …”. It implies what (in terms of clues or evidences) will give you an idea about what the true reality of something is.
- When the present/future tense of this expression is used in the Qur’an مَا يُدْرِيكَ the answer and further elaboration is not provided. But when the past tense is used like it is here مَا أَدْرَاكَ the answer and further elaboration is provided.
Derivative: رَقَبَةٍ refers to the neck or the back of the neck. It is used in the Arabic language to refer to a slave as his neck is chained.

Derivative: فَكُّ is the Masdar of فَكَّ – يَفُكُّ which means to undo, unbind, dislodge or free something from something. It is used an expression for freeing a slave due to the undoing of the binds or chains of slavery. The use of the Masdar فَكُّ instead of the verb يَفُكُّ here indicates that this is not a one-off action but rather something that someone is committed to undertaking.
Derivative: مَسْغَبَةٍ from سَغَبَ which means to be afflicted by hunger. مَسْغَبَةٍ refers to a general state of hunger like a famine.

Derivative: إِطْعَامٌ means to feed or provide food and is from the verb أَطْعَمَ – يُطْعِمُ. The use of the noun instead of the verb indicates to a commitment to the act and that is not just a one-time action to be undertaken.

Derivative: مَتْرَبَةٍ is in the مَصْدَر مِيمِى (Madar Meemiy) form which is stronger in import than the normal Masdar. It means to have dust or dirt attached to him and is an expression of being in a state of severe need.
Derivative: مِسْكِينًا comes from مَسْكَنَة which refers to a state of impoverishment. The مِسْكِين is the one who barely has the essentials of life to survive.
Derivative: الْمَرْحَمَةِ is in the مَصْدَر مِيمِى (Madar Meemiy) form which makes it wider and more powerful than رَحْمَة (Rahmah) and means mercy for everyone and everything i.e., towards creation.
Derivative: الصَّبْرِ (Sabr) encompasses the meanings of patience, commitment, consistency and constancy. The last three meanings can fall under the meaning of perseverance.

Derivative: تَوَاصَوْا: وَصِيَة (Wasiyah) refers to a will, directive, instruction or legacy i.e., something that is left for others to follow. تَواصَى – تَواصٍ means to urge, enjoin, motivate and advise one another to undertake a certain course of action.
- This statement can have two possible meanings: 1) “They enjoin one another to have patience” or 2) “They enjoin one another with patience” i.e., they have patience when they enjoin one another”.

- “The companions (أَصْحَابُ) of the right (الْمَيْمَنَةِ)”. Here الْمَيْمَنَةِ is in the مَصْدَر مِيمِى (Madar Meemiy) form which is wider and more powerful in import than the regular Masdar. It therefore implies far out to the right i.e., safe on the right side and is more powerful than the statement أَصْحابُ اليَمِين (As’haab ul-Yameen) “The companions of the right”.
Derivative: آيَاتِ is the plural of آَيَة and refers to Allah’s miraculous signs. It can be in reference to His revelations (i.e., the Qur’an) and also to His creation.

- “The companions (أَصْحَابُ) of الْمَشْأَمَةِ (Al-Mash’amah” – الْمَشْأَمَةِ comes from شُؤًم (Shu’um) which refers to an evil omen and misfortune. مَشْأَمَة (Mash’amah) also refers to the left (side) in the case where the left side was associated with what is bad and the right with what is good. As such أَصْحَابُ الْمَشْأَمَةِ (As’haab ul-Mash’amah) can either refer to the people of great misfortune (i.e., wretched and cursed) or the people who are far out to the left side.

Derivative: حِلٌّ comes from حَلَّ or أَحَلَّ in the Arabic language and it can mean a number of things here. One meaning is to “be deemed Halaal” in the sense that the Messenger ﷺ has been deemed permissible for the Quraish to harm i.e., a legitimate target and prey. Another meaning is “to be free to act and dispose as you please” i.e., it is Halaal for you to do so and have been provided with a permissibility that is special to you. The word حِلٌّ can also carry the meaning of arriving or descending at a place and settling and residing in it. The original meaning of حَلَّ is to untie something and was used when explaining the untying of saddlebags when intending to descend and stay at a place. The meaning in this case would be that you are “a permanent fixture or inhabitant” of this city of Makkah.

Derivative: مُؤْصَدَةٌ comes from أَوْصَدَ - إِيْصاد which means to seal something up with something else. It implies to be firmly closed shut, like a lid or seal that has been locked in place or a door that has been firmly shut, from which there is no escape.

وَوَالِدٍ وَمَا وَلَدَ

- This is connected to the oath in the first verse “No! I swear by this city … And I swear by a father (وَالِدٍ) and what was born (of him) (وَمَا وَلَدَ)”.
- The majority opinion is that وَالِدٍ (Waalid - Father) refers to Adam (as) and وَمَا وَلَدَ (what was born) refers to his progeny. It has also been said that it refers to Ibrahim (as) and his progeny which has a direct link and relevance to هَٰذَا الْبَلَدِ (this city i.e., Makkah) mentioned in the initial oath which is linked to this oath.

Derivative: كَبَدٍ refers to an intensely laboursome toil and exhaustion which overwhelms a person from every direction i.e., the human has been created in a state of having to endure an intense, laboursome and exhausting struggle.

لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسَانَ فِي كَبَدٍ

- “And We have indeed created (لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا) the human being (الْإِنْسَانَ) in a state of Kabad (فِي كَبَدٍ)” – This is the جَوَابُ القَسْم (Jawaab ul-Qasm – Answer to the preceding oath and its subject).
Derivative: لُبَدًا refers to that which is stuck and piled together due to its great amount. Here it means in great quantities, one piled mass after another.

- أَهْلَكْتُ مَالًا literally means “I have destroyed wealth” and it means that I have wasted and squandered it. It is an expression of arrogance and vain boasting. Using the word أَهْلَكْتُ “I have destroyed or brought ruin” to refer to spending implies that the spending was a ruinous and destructive spending in terms of it being in sinful things and activities that lead to one’s ruin and not spending in that which leads to one’s success.
